---
title: "Historical Trends and Emerging Environmental Pressures on Nursery Function in French Estuaries: An Integrated Approach Combining Contaminants, Water Quality, Benthic Fauna and Fish Communities"
documentclass: article
link-citations: yes
author:
  - Florence MOUNIER:
      email: florence.moounier@inrae.fr
      institute: [inrae]
      correspondence: true
  - Henrique Cabral:
      institute: [inrae]
  - Jérémy Lobry:
      institute: [inrae]
institute:
  - inrae: INRAE, UR EABX, 50 avenue de Verdun, 33612 Cestas Cedex, France
output: 
  officedown::rdocx_document:
    reference_docx: template.docx
    tables:
      caption:
        style: "Table Caption"
    plots:
      caption:
        style: "Figure caption"
    pandoc_args:
      - '--lua-filter=scholarly-metadata.lua'
      - '--lua-filter=author-info-blocks.lua'
bibliography: references.bib
number-sections: true
csl: "aquatic-living-resources.csl"
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  error = FALSE,
  fig.width = 7,
  fig.height = 5,
  out.width = "95%"
)
library(esteem.overview)
library(officer)
library(tidyverse)
library(flextable)
```

##### Abstract

##### keywords

nursery quality, global changes, estuaries

\newpage

# Introduction

@elliottEstuarineQualityParadox2007 proposed the concept of the estuarine quality paradox, highlighting that estuaries simultaneously represent highly productive and functionally important ecosystems—particularly as nursery habitats for fish—while being among the most anthropogenically degraded environments. They argue that conventional approaches to ecological quality assessment, often based on deviation from near-pristine reference conditions, are poorly suited to estuaries, which are inherently dynamic and naturally variable systems. Instead, estuarine ecological “quality” should be interpreted in terms of ecosystem functioning, particularly its capacity to support key biological processes such as growth and survival of juvenile fish, rather than solely on physico-chemical status.

Diagnostic basé sur l’étude des paramètres impliqués dans la qualité de la fonctionnalité de nourricerie des estuaires : paramètres physico-chimiques, contamination, diversité et qualité écologique des communautés de faune benthique et de poissons (+ indices de condition poisson ?)

Comparaison de la qualité de la fonctionnalité de nourriceries des trois plus grands estuaires français (Gironde, Loire et Seine) et de leurs tendances d’évolution à long terme et sur les 20 dernières années.

« La fonction de nourricerie est l’une des principales assurées par les estuaires de la Mer du Nord et ceux de l’Atlantique européen [@whitfield2002]( ; Franco et al, 2008). Une fraction importante de l'ichtyofaune estuarienne est originaire du milieu marin et constitue la guilde des espèces migrantes marines, qui naissent en mer et peuvent pénétrer dans les estuaires de façon plus ou moins régulière puis retournent en mer pour leurs stades ultérieurs de développement. Le bar (Dicentrarchus labrax) et la sole (Solea solea), d'origine marine, dépendent plus étroitement au stage juvénile des habitats abrités estuariens, avec des niveaux d’abondance pouvant être très élevés pour les stades juvéniles. Ce sont ces espèces de la guilde des espèces marines migrantes estuariennes-dépendantes (MMD) que nous avons choisies pour représenter les habitats de la fonction de nourricerie de poissons marins. Nous nous sommes particulièrement focalisés sur les groupes d’âge G0 (1ère année de vie) et G1 (2ème année de vie) de ces espèces, correspondants aux stades de vie pour lesquels les estuaires sont des habitats préférentiels (Bacq et al., 2013).

An integrated methodology for assessment of estuarine trophic status <https://www-sciencedirect-com.inrae.idm.oclc.org/science/article/pii/S0304380003001996>

River discharge is a key driver of estuarine nursery habitat functioning, as it regulates salinity gradients, turbidity, and productivity (@baptistaEffectsFreshwaterFlow2010). Thereby, it influences recruitment and settlement (e.g., olfactory cues for fish larvae in the river plumes), and  habitat suitability for juvenile fish (e.g., turbid water protect juveniles from predators). In temperate estuaries, its effects are strongly season-dependent: winter discharge contributes to the physical structuring of the estuary, spring discharge modulates habitat conditions during the main recruitment period of marine juvenile fishes (@arevaloFishLarvaeDynamics2023) such as common sole and European seabass, and summer discharge extremes may induce physiological stress (e.g., increases in salinity and decreases in dissolved oxygen) or reduce juvenile retention within nursery areas. These seasonal dynamics justify a temporally explicit analysis of discharge effects when assessing estuarine nursery quality.

# Materials and Methods

## Datasets

Physico-chemical, macrobenthic, fish, shrimps, and legacy biota contamination data were obtained from Ifremer’s Quadrige database, which is freely accessible via [surval.ifremer.fr](surval.ifremer.fr). This database compiles data from various monitoring and research programs at a broad spatial scale. The extracted data correspond to the following Quadrige marine areas: “085 – Gironde Estuary,” “070 – Loire Estuary,” and “011 – Seine Estuary.”

Estuarine intertidal surface areas data were obtained from the study of @lecuyerThrivingLifeBiodiversity2024a for the Loire and the Seine estuary, and @sottolichioEvaluationRecentMorphological2013 and @blanchet2018habitats for the Gironde estuary.

River flows datasets were exported from [Hydroportail v3.4.5](https://www.hydro.eaufrance.fr/).

Yearly monitoring design and completion of data for the different biotic and abiotic studied variables is presented for each estuary in Figure \@ref(fig:data_design).

```{r data_design, fig.cap="Yearly monitoring design for the different biotic and abiotic studied variables by estuary.", fig.show='hold'}
knitr::include_graphics(get_shortened_path("mat_meth/ggplot_data_design.jpg"))
```

### Physico-chemical parameters

#### Monitoring programs

The selected physico-chemical data originate from the following monitoring programs:  RNOHYD before early 2000's, and REPHY and RESLOC_44 after that and util 2024 (see the Supplementary Information section entitled “Monitoring programs” for their quick presentation). All these programs perform only one measurement per month at each sampling site; however, not all months of the year are systematically covered by sampling campaigns.

Thus, the variables and sampling stations were selected to obtain annual data over the longest possible time period across all estuaries, within a restricted spatial extent of each estuary. The selected sampling stations are presented along with their corresponding monitoring programs and measurement periods in the Supplementary Information section entitled “Selected physico-chemical location points”. The selected variables include temperature, salinity, and nitrogen compounds (ammonium NH₄⁺, nitrate NO3-, and nitrite NO2-). The selected study period spans 1987–2024 and focuses exclusively on autumn. Monthly values were averaged over September and October for temperature and salinity, and over September, October, and November for nitrogen compounds.

Temperature sensors at the selected sampling stations were deployed between 0 and 12 m depth. For salinity, _in situ_ conductivity sensors (REPHY; RHLN) were positioned between 10 and 15 m, while samples analyzed using the Knudsen titration method (RNOHYD) were collected between 2 and 12 m. Nitrogen compounds were measured between 0.5 and 1 m depth. Ammonium was measured by spectrometry or fluorimetry, whereas nitrate and nitrite were measured by spectrophotometry, chromatography, or colorimetry.

Based on measurements of the different nitrogen compounds, a nitrification indicator was calculated as the ratio of ammonium to total dissolved inorganic nitrogen: $NH₄⁺ / (NH₄⁺ + NO₃⁻ + NO₂⁻)$. This ratio is used to characterize the dominant form of available nitrogen and provides insight into nitrogen cycle processes (regeneration, nitrification, anthropogenic inputs). In estuarine environments, this indicator is particularly relevant, as these systems act as biogeochemical reactors where nitrogen undergoes intense transformations.


The sampling stations were mapped onto estuary charts in the following figures:
```{r fig.cap="Physico-chemical sampling points in the Gironde estuary.", fig.width=6, fig.height=4}
knitr::include_graphics(
  get_shortened_path("mat_meth/maps/PHY_CHEM/Temperature/ggplot_Temperature_Gironde_map.jpg")
)
```

```{r fig.cap="Physico-chemical sampling points in the Loire estuary.", tafig.width=6, fig.height=4}
knitr::include_graphics(
  get_shortened_path("mat_meth/maps/PHY_CHEM/Temperature/ggplot_Temperature_Loire_map.jpg")
)
```

```{r fig.cap="Physico-chemical sampling points in the Seine estuary.", fig.width=6, fig.height=4}
knitr::include_graphics(
  get_shortened_path("mat_meth/maps/PHY_CHEM/Temperature/ggplot_Temperature_Seine_map.jpg")
)
```

### River discharge

River discharge was included as a temporally structured explanatory variable to account for its seasonal influence on estuarine nursery functioning. 

Data were obtained from Hydroportail (v3.4.5; [https://www.hydro.eaufrance.fr/](https://www.hydro.eaufrance.fr/)). For the Gironde estuary, the selected discharge was calculated as the sum of flows from the two upstream rivers, using measurements from Pessac-sur-Dordogne (Dordogne River) and Tonneins (Garonne River). For the Loire and Seine estuaries, upstream discharges were derived from measurements at Montjean-sur-Loire and Vernon, respectively.

Following established conceptual frameworks in estuarine fish ecology, discharge was partitioned into three biologically relevant seasonal windows corresponding to distinct mechanisms of influence on juvenile fish habitats.

Autumn discharge (September-October-November)

Winter discharge (December–January-February) was used as an indicator of estuarine pre-conditioning, influencing large-scale physical structuring processes such as salinity gradient formation, sediment resuspension, and turbidity regimes prior to the recruitment period.

Spring discharge (March-April–May), corresponding to the main recruitment period of common sole and European seabass juveniles in temperate estuaries, was used as the primary indicator of habitat conditions during settlement and early post-settlement phases. This period is critical for determining food availability, growth conditions, and habitat suitability within nursery areas.

Summer discharge (June-July–August) was included to capture potential stress conditions affecting juveniles already established in estuarine habitats. During this period, low-flow conditions may exacerbate hypoxia and thermal stress, while high-flow events may alter salinity regimes and reduce juvenile retention within nursery habitats.

For each year over 1975–2024, mean discharge was calculated for each seasonal window (from daily or monthly data) and used as separate explanatory variables in subsequent analyses of estuarine nursery quality.

```{r fig.cap="Autumn river flows.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
knitr::include_graphics(
  get_shortened_path("mat_meth/ggplot_data_flow_autumn.jpg")
)
```


### Intertidal surface area

Intertidal surface area is directly related to the space available for the development of benthic communities. Long-term records of intertidal surface area, including estimates for each haline sector, were obtained from the study by @lecuyerThrivingLifeBiodiversity2024a for the Loire (1977–2012) and Seine (1970–2011) estuaries, and from @sottolichioEvaluationRecentMorphological2013 (1970–1994) and @blanchet2018habitats (2011) for the Gironde estuary.

In @lecuyerThrivingLifeBiodiversity2024a, surface areas were calculated from digitizations carried out using various historical cartographic sources. In @sottolichioEvaluationRecentMorphological2013, lateral surface areas were derived from between the 0 isobath and the coastline based on historical bathymetrics. In both studies, estimates of intertidal surface area were obtained independently of habitat type or substrate composition. Consequently, mudflats and sandbanks were aggregated and considered as a single intertidal area metric.

In contrast, @blanchet2018habitats derived intertidal surface area from habitat mapping. Total intertidal area was estimated by summing the surface areas of the two EUNIS intertidal sediment habitat classes present in the Gironde estuary: A2.2 (Intertidal sand and muddy sand) and A2.3 (Intertidal mud).

```{r fig.cap="Estimated intertidal surface areas.", fig.width=6, fig.height=4}
knitr::include_graphics(
  get_shortened_path("mat_meth/ggplot_intertidal_areas.jpg")
)
```

### Contamination of biota by legislated compounds

#### Monitoring program

As part of the ROCCHMV monitoring program, concentrations of various contaminants were measured in bivalves collected over the period 1979 to 2024. In the Gironde estuary, the Pacific oysters (_Crassostrea gigas_) was monitored. In the Seine estuary, the blue mussel (_Mytilus edulis_) was studied. In the Loire estuary, the blue mussel was initially monitored, and, from 2017 onward, oysters were also included in parallel. Sampling was conducted in the most saline zones of each estuary, near their respective mouths (Figure \@ref(fig:map_phychem)).

```{r map_phychem, fig.cap="Map of sampling points for the ROCCHMV program.", fig.show='hold'}
knitr::include_graphics(get_shortened_path("mat_meth/maps/ROCCHMV/ggmap_ROCCHMV_gironde.jpg"))
knitr::include_graphics(get_shortened_path("mat_meth/maps/ROCCHMV/ggmap_ROCCHMV_loire.jpg"))
knitr::include_graphics(get_shortened_path("mat_meth/maps/ROCCHMV/ggmap_ROCCHMV_seine.jpg"))
```

#### Data transformations

When multiple measurements were available for the same year, the annual median value was used. Values under limit of detection were replaced by zero value. The similarity in bioaccumulation potential between mussels and oysters was assessed based on differences in their contamination levels. Where differences were observed, such as those documented for several metals [@ifremerQualiteMilieuMarin2024], all concentrations were expressed in “mussel equivalents” using an oyster-to-mussel conversion factor. This factor was calculated for each metal as the ratio of median contamination levels in oysters to those in mussels, based on seven years (2017-2024) of parallel monitoring in the Loire estuary. The resulting conversion factors were 0.634 for mercury, 0.718 for cadmium, 0.898 for lead, and 0.036 for copper.

#### Studied compounds

The full list of contaminants monitored under the ROCCHMV program is available on the “Envlit” website maintained by Ifremer (<https://envlit.ifremer.fr/Surveillance-du-littoral/Contaminants-chimiques/Contaminants-suivis>). For the present study, a subset of compounds was selected and is listed in Table 1, based on the following criteria: (1) inclusion in European or national regulatory framework with established thresholds for human health and ecotoxicological risk in biota [@europeancommissionDIRECTIVE2013392013; @legifranceArticle3Larrete2023; @osparStatusTrendHeavy2023; @osparStatusTrendsConcentrations2023; @osparStatusTrendsLevels2023; @osparStatusTrendsPolychlorinated2023]; and (2) detection above analytical limits in the present dataset. These criteria ensure that the selected compounds are ecologically and toxicologically relevant and reflect the contamination levels to which juvenile organisms and their prey are exposed.

Table \@ref(tab:tab-contam-rocchmv) summarizes the main sources and ecotoxicological effects as synthetized from (Ifremer, 2021). The table also presents the relevant legislative thresholds, specifying their regulatory context (human health or ecotoxicology), the biological matrix of reference (primarily bivalves or fish), and the legislative source.

Three legislated heavy metals have been studied (mercury, cadmium, and lead). Copper was also included due to its historical significance as a pollutant in the Gironde estuary (Ifremer, 2024). Two types of organochlorine (OC) pesticides are under legislative thresholds: γ-HCH (or Lindane) and so called DDT total (sum of isomers p,p'-DDT (4,4’-DDT), o,p’-DDT (2,4’-DDT), p,p'-DDE (4,4’-DDE), p,p'-DDD (4,4’-DDD)). Eight Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon (PAH) are under OSPAR legislation: fluoranthene, benzo(a)pyrene (abr. benzo-pyr.), anthracene, naphthalene, phenanthrene, pyrene, benzo(a)anthracene (abr. benzo-anthra.), and benzo(g,h,i)perylene (abr. benzo-peryl.). Seven indicator congeners of the polychlorinated biphenyls (PCB) family are under OSPAR legislation: CB 28, CB 52, CB 101, CB 118, CB 138, CB 153, CB 180. Dioxins and dioxin-like compounds are under a European Commission legislation under the form of a weighted sum of PCDD + PCDF + PCB-DL expressed in toxic equivalents concentrations (see SI for details). Two types of brominated flame retardant were studied: the three isomers of HBCDD (hexabromocyclododecane) and the sum of Polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDE) congeners 28, 47, 99, 100, 153 and 154. Two emergent compounds were studied more recently: a perfluorinated compound (perfluorooctane sulfonate, PFOS), and an organostannic compound (tributyltin cation).

```{r tab-contam-rocchmv, tab.cap="Studied Compounds in the ROCCHMV Program: Contaminant Families, Principal Sources, Available Time Series, Considered Measurement Matrices, and Sources of Regulatory Thresholds. Thresholds related to human health: QS(HH) (EU Quality Standard for Human Health); EC MPC (European Commission Maximum Permissible Concentrations). Thresholds related to ecotoxicology: EQS (Environmental Quality Standard); EAC (Environmental Assessment Criteria; OSPAR equivalent of EQS for the North-East Atlantic); NQE (Norme de Qualité Environnementale; French equivalent of EQS); VGE (Valeurs Guide Environnementales; French EQS equivalent for bivalves). Matrix abbreviations: f., fish; c., crustaceans; m., molluscs; b., bivalves.", out.width="90%", fig.show='hold'}
knitr::include_graphics(get_shortened_path("mat_meth/contamination/table_rocchmv.jpg"))
```

### Recent contamination of biota and sea water by emerging biocides

As part of the revision of the national list of Specific Pollutants of Ecological Status (PSEE), aimed at identifying relevant substances for monitoring in coastal environments, Ifremer investigated 102 emerging organic substances (i.e., not currently regulated for the marine environment), including different types of biocides [@amourouxEmergentSeaRechercheSubstances2025]. These measurements were carried out in 2021 and 2022, alongside ROCCHMV sampling, using integrative matrices—namely, bivalves and passive samplers (POCIS)—to facilitate the detection of these substances. The use of passive samplers is particularly suited for targeting organic compounds with low hydrophobicity, which are expected to exhibit limited bioconcentration and bioaccumulation in marine organisms. In addition, the compounds studied are generally less persistent than those subject to regulation, making them more susceptible to seasonal variation.

Sampling sites and periods were selected to coincide with locations and times of known contaminant inputs (i.e., related to usage patterns). The emerging compounds selected for this study include those quantified in at least one of the three estuary (Seine: Villerville site; Loire: Montoir-Ponton site; Gironde: Pontaillac site) and quantified in seawater (2 herbicides: AMPA, and Glyphosate) or in bivalves (7 antifouling agents: DCOIT, Dichlofluanide, Irgarol, Medetomidine, and Tralopyril; 7 fungicides: Azoxystrobin, Boscalid, Chlorothalonil, Propiconazole, Thirame, and Tebuconazole; 1 insecticide: Fipronil; and 14 herbicides: 3,4-DCA, Chlorprophame, Chlortoluron, DCPMU, DCPU, DEA, Diflufenican, Dimethenamide, Diuron, Linuron, Metoxuron, Metalochlor, Propazine, and Terbuthylazine desethyl). 

To determine whether the estuaries were more contaminated than the French coastline overall, annual mean concentrations were calculated at the national level and compared with the corresponding yearly means observed in each estuary. To assess the potential environmental risk associated with these contamination levels, observed concentrations were also compared to Predicted No Effect Concentration (PNEC) values. Another way to evaluate whether contamination levels exceeding the national average may reflect an environmental risk was to consider the ranking of the compounds in the Emergent’Sea program. This ranking is based on the frequency of quantification of each compound, as well as the extent and frequency of PNEC exceedances.


### Macrobenthic fauna densities

Benthic invertebrate fauna were investigated using data from two monitoring programs. The REBENT benthic network (“Réseau de surveillance benthique”), also implemented within the WFD-Benthos framework under the European Water Framework Directive (WFD), is a French monitoring program dedicated to benthic macrofauna in coastal and transitional waters. This program aims to assess the ecological quality of marine and coastal environments based on benthic community structure, which is sensitive to pressures such as eutrophication, pollution, sediment disturbance, and climate change (@blanchetMethodologiePourSurveillance).

However, only a single sampling year was available for the Loire estuary within the REBENT dataset (see years of REBENT macrobenthic survey campaigns for the Gironde, Loire, and Seine estuaries in \@ref(fig:biota-exp-plan)). To investigate temporal changes in macrobenthic species densities in the Loire estuary, we therefore used data from a comparable monitoring program conducted by EDF and INRAE. This program monitors the aquatic environment associated with the operation of the Cordemais power plant and has been conducted annually from 2008 to 2024. Its objective is to assess the impact of cooling-water discharges in the vicinity of the power plant, in accordance with regulatory requirements for hydrobiological environmental monitoring.

Both programs used 1-mm square mesh sieves. To maximize temporal consistency among monitoring programs and estuaries, only autumn surveys were retained to generate annual datasets (September–November for the REBENT program and October–November for the EDF program).

Raw data consist of species-specific macrobenthic densities (number of individuals per square metre) measured over time. These data can be used to investigate temporal changes in community taxonomic composition, particularly among major taxonomic groups such as polychaetes, molluscs, and crustaceans. The dominant macrobenthic species can also be assessed in relation to the preferred prey of the common sole, providing insight into potential changes in food resource availability.


### Fish and shrimp densities, and associated physico-chemical parameters

Fish data were collected within the Water Framework Directive (WFD; 2000/60/EC) monitoring program established for water quality assessment. The WFD requires Member States to monitor aquatic ecosystems, including estuarine environments. To meet these requirements, a standardized methodology based on estuarine fishing surveys was developed in the early 2000s.

Sampling campaigns have been conducted since 2005 in 32 French estuaries during both spring and autumn to estimate fish and shrimp densities, together with physicochemical parameters such as salinity. According to the monitoring design, surveys are theoretically carried out over three consecutive years within each six-year management cycle (see years of WFD fishing survey campaigns for the Gironde, Loire, and Seine estuaries in \@ref(fig:data-design)).  For each campaign (i.e., one estuary, one year, and one season), fish abundance was estimated using beam trawl surveys following the protocol described in @onemaReseauxControleSurveillance2014.

Briefly, two types of beam trawls were used depending on estuary size: (i) a trawl 2.9 m wide and 0.5 m high with a 20 mm stretched mesh cod-end for large estuaries, and (ii) a trawl 1.5 m wide and 0.5 m high with a 16 mm stretched mesh cod-end for small estuaries, in accordance with the standardized French estuarine protocol (NF T90-701, implemented on 2021-07-01). Trawling was conducted during daytime at speeds ranging from 1.5 to 3 knots for approximately 15 minutes. A minimum of eight samples were collected within each salinity zone (oligohaline, mesohaline, and polyhaline). Salinity, temperature and dioxygen saturation was also measured during trawl surveys.

## Computation of complementary parameters for biotic evolution analysis

#### Haline zones definition
 
To investigate long-term changes in biotic and abiotic parameters along estuarine salinity gradients, two haline zones were defined in each estuary using salinity data collected during autumn WFD fish surveys over the last 20 years. Regression trees were fitted with salinity as the response variable and geographic coordinates as predictors (`rpart()` function, {rpart} package). The first split of each tree, corresponding to the geographic threshold maximizing salinity differences between groups, was retained as the boundary between upstream and downstream salinity zones. (see the Supplementary Information section entitled “Haline zones definition” for map representations and salinity distributions).

#### Macrobenthic BEQI-FR quality index

Macrobenthic community composition may influence the nursery quality of habitats for common sole and European seabass through at least two pathways. First, benthic taxa differ in their vulnerability to predation by these species, thereby affecting food availability. Second, taxa exhibit contrasting sensitivities to pollution and organic enrichment, making community composition a useful indicator of environmental quality. To integrate this ecological dimension, we calculated the French Benthic Ecosystem Quality Index (BEQI-FR), which assesses the capacity of benthic communities to resist anthropogenic disturbance. BEQI-FR is the indicator currently used to evaluate the ecological status of estuarine transitional waters in mainland France (@fouetSelectionDunIndicateur2018; @fouetIntercalibrationBiologicalElements2020).

The index combines the AZTI Marine Biotic Index (AMBI; @borjaMarineBioticIndex2000a; @borjaGuidelinesUseAMBI2005a), species richness ((S)), and the Shannon diversity index ((H')). Observed values are standardized against habitat-specific reference values according to:

$$
\mathrm{BEQI\mbox{-}FR}
=
\frac{
\dfrac{S_{\mathrm{obs}}}{S_{\mathrm{ref}}}
+
\dfrac{H'_{\mathrm{obs}}}{H'_{\mathrm{ref}}}
+
\dfrac{\mathrm{AMBI}_{\mathrm{obs}} - 7}{\mathrm{AMBI}_{\mathrm{ref}} - 7}
}{3}
$$
Observed AMBI values were calculated following the methodology described by @blanchetMethodologiePourSurveillance. Species are first assigned to one of five ecological groups reflecting their sensitivity to organic enrichment and pollution: GI (sensitive species), GII (indifferent species), GIII (tolerant species), GIV (second-order opportunistic species), and GV (first-order opportunistic species). AMBI is then calculated using the ambi() function from the {benthos} package according to:

$$
\mathrm{AMBI} = \frac{0\,G_{I} + 1.5\,G_{II} + 3\,G_{III} + 4.5\,G_{IV} + 6\,G_{V}}{100}
$$
To determine the reference values $S_{\mathrm{ref}}$, $H'{\mathrm{ref}}$, and $\mathrm{AMBI}{\mathrm{ref}}$ following @fouetSelectionDunIndicateur2018, intertidal and subtidal habitats were first classified according to the EUNIS habitat typology. Habitat classification was based on dominant sediment grain size, sediment organic matter content, and the dominant benthic taxa present. The resulting habitat classifications, together with the corresponding reference values, are provided in the Supplementary Information section entitled “Benthic quality index computation details”.

The Loire, Gironde, and Seine estuaries are classified as large estuaries (Type D) under the French assessment framework. Consequently, BEQI-FR ecological quality ratios (EQRs) were interpreted using the classification thresholds shown in Table \@ref(tab:tab_EQR_BEQI). Following @blanchetMethodologiePourSurveillance2025, EQR values exceeding 1 were truncated to 1.

```{r}
beqi_classes <- tribble(
  ~`BEQI-FR`, ~High, ~Good, ~Moderate, ~Poor, ~Bad,
  "EQR boundaries", "]1–0.86]", "]0.86–0.67]", "]0.67–0.40]", "]0.40–0.20]", "]0.20–0]"
)

tab_EQR_BEQI <- flextable(beqi_classes) |>
  align(j = 1, align = "left") |>
  align(j = 2:6, align = "center") |>
  bold(part = "header") |>
  autofit()

tab_EQR_BEQI <- set_caption(
  x = tab_EQR_BEQI,
  caption = "BEQI-FR EQR thresholds for large estuaries (type D).",
  autonum = run_autonum(
    seq_id = "tab",
    bkm = "tab_EQR_BEQI"
  )
)

tab_EQR_BEQI
```

#### Fish size class


## Time-series analysis

### Abiotic long term trends analysis

Long-term analyses were conducted on physicochemical parameters monitored over the period 1987–2024, river discharge data available from 1975–2024, legislated contaminants monitored from at least 1995 to 2023 or 2024, and intertidal surface areas documented from as early as 1841 in the Seine estuary until 2011 or 2012, depending on the estuary.

#### Individual parameter analyses

For each time series, temporal dynamics were visualized using a LOESS (locally estimated scatterplot smoothing) curve fitted with the geom_smooth() function from the {ggplot2} package in R. This non-parametric smoothing approach was used to highlight long-term patterns while reducing short-term variability.

For time series displaying an apparent monotonic trend, the significance of the trend was assessed using the modified Mann–Kendall test for autocorrelated data, implemented with the mmkh() function from the {modifiedmk} package. Depending on whether the trend was identified as monotonic or non-monotonic, different analytical approaches were applied.

For significantly monotonic trends, trend magnitude was estimated using the Theil–Sen slope estimator, computed with the sens.slope() function from the {trend} package. In addition, differences between the beginning and the end of the study period were evaluated by comparing observations from the first and last ten years of each time series using a Mann–Whitney U test (Wilcoxon rank-sum test), implemented with the wilcox.test() function in R.

For legislated contaminants exhibiting a significant decreasing trend, the time required for concentrations to fall below the Environmental Quality Standard (EQS) was estimated assuming a constant linear rate of decline derived from the Theil–Sen slope estimate. This approach provided an approximate projection of the time required to achieve compliance with the EQS.

For time series displaying either a visually non-monotonic pattern or a non-significant Mann–Kendall trend, structural changes were investigated using change-point analysis. Breakpoints associated with changes in both mean and variance were identified using the cpt.meanvar() function from the {changepoint} package.

#### Relationships among parameters

Relationships among abiotic parameters were explored using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) performed on the common monitoring period (1987–2024), during which all variables were available. PCA was conducted using the {FactoMineR} package.

Occasional missing values were handled using the {missMDA} package, which performs iterative imputation based on PCA. Briefly, missing values were initially replaced by variable means and subsequently updated through successive PCA iterations until convergence, thereby minimizing the distance between observations and their projections in the multivariate space.

Intertidal surface area data contained too few observations to allow reliable PCA-based imputation. To retain these variables in the analysis, missing values occurring between observations were interpolated using a monotonic cubic spline (splinefun(method = "monoH.FC")). For years beyond the last observation, values were extrapolated assuming continuation of the long-term monotonic trend, using a linear projection based on the Theil–Sen slope estimator (sens.slope() function from the {trend} package).

### Biotic and abiotic recent trends analysis

Recent trends were studied from the year of the first and the last biotic data from the WDF monitoring programs (i.e., 2025). A PCA was performed


<!-- Recent trends were estimated using the last 15 years of time series data, applying Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient (ρ). Statistical significance of the correlations was tested using the cor.test function from the {stats} package in R. For each estuary and each compound, the correlation coefficient and its associated p-value were used to evaluate the presence and direction of recent trends. -->



# Results

## Haline zones

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("mat_meth/maps/POMET/haline_zones/ggplot_POMET_haline_zones_estuaries.png")
)
```

## PCA on physico-chemical variables by estuary

### Gironde

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/PCA/Gironde/ggplot_biplot_PCA.jpg")
)
```

### Loire

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/PCA/Loire/ggplot_biplot_PCA.jpg")
)
```

### Seine

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/PCA/Seine/ggplot_biplot_PCA.jpg")
)
```

## Physico-chemical variables dynamics by estuary

### Temperature

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Temperature/ggplot_Temperature_Gironde_trend.jpg")
)
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Temperature/ggplot_Temperature_Loire_trend.jpg")
)
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Temperature/ggplot_Temperature_Seine_trend.jpg")
)
```

### Salinity

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Salinity/ggplot_Salinity_Gironde_trend.jpg")
)
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Salinity/ggplot_Salinity_Loire_trend.jpg")
)
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Salinity/ggplot_Salinity_Seine_trend.jpg")
)
```

### Ammonium proportion

Nitrification is the microbially catalyzed oxidation of ammonia (NH3) or reduced ammonium (NH4+) to nitrite (NO2 −), followed by oxidation of NO2 − to nitrate (NO3 −). (Damashek, J., Casciotti, K.L. & Francis, C.A. Variable Nitrification Rates Across Environmental Gradients in Turbid, Nutrient-Rich Estuary Waters of San Francisco Bay. Estuaries and Coasts 39, 1050–1071 (2016). <https://doi.org/10.1007/s12237-016-0071-7>)

remineralization: phytoplankton and other heterotrophic bacteria break down nitrite into reduced nitrate and ammonium

Ammonium ++ = sources terrestres d'azote OU forte activité du phytoplancton

NH₄⁺/DIN = NH₄⁺ / (NH₄⁺ + NO₃⁻+ NO₂⁻)

Le DIN (Dissolved Inorganic Nitrogen) correspond à la somme des formes inorganiques dissoutes de l’azote. Cet indicateur permet d’évaluer la part relative de l’azote réduit (NH₄⁺) par rapport aux formes oxydées (NO₃⁻, NO₂⁻). Les concentrations élevées d’ammonium dans les estuaires eutrophes (MO +++) stimulent fortement la nitrification (Damashek, J., Casciotti, K.L. & Francis, C.A. Variable Nitrification Rates Across Environmental Gradients in Turbid, Nutrient-Rich Estuary Waters of San Francisco Bay. Estuaries and Coasts 39, 1050–1071 (2016). <https://doi.org/10.1007/s12237-016-0071-7>)

-   \< 0.1 : système dominé par nitrate (apports fluviaux agriculture/bassin versant, nitrification très efficace car assimilation de l'ammonium par le phytoplankton, dilution marine)

-   [0.1-0.3] : situation fréquente en estuaire modérément productif

-   [0.3-0.6] : forte régénération ou influence anthropique

-   

    > 0.6 : dominance ammonium (forte décomposition de matière organique libérant de l'ammonium, hypoxie ou turbidité élevée empêchant l'activité de nitrification du phytoplancton recyclage intense)

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Nitrogen_indicator/ggplot_Nitrogen_indicator_Gironde_trend.jpg")
)
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Nitrogen_indicator/ggplot_Nitrogen_indicator_Loire_trend.jpg")
)
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(
get_shortened_path("results/data_phychem/Nitrogen_indicator/ggplot_Nitrogen_indicator_Seine_trend.jpg")
)
```

## Contamination

### Legislated compounds



### Emerging biocides

Among the 31 emergent biocides assessed, eight exceeded their respective PNEC in at least one estuary. Two compounds, diflufenican and fipronil, exceeded the PNEC by more than a factor of four in all three estuaries. These substances are ranked first and second in the Emergent’Sea program’s monitoring prioritization. The fact that fipronil and diflufenican are not systematically detected at higher concentrations in estuaries compared to the national average suggests that PNEC exceedances are also frequent along other parts of the French coastline, justifying their high prioritization.

```{r tab-res-contam-biocides, tab.cap="Emerging biocides studied in the Gironde, Loire and Seine estuaries under the Emergent’Sea research program along with their PNEC (in μg/kg_ww for biota, in μg/L for POCIS) values. Colors correspond to deviation from PNEC value (white: [0-100%[, yellow:[100-200%[, orange: [200-300%[, red: [300-400%[, black: > 400%). The ranking indicates the degree of priorisation of the substance defined by the Emergent’Sea program.", out.width="90%", fig.show='hold'}
knitr::include_graphics(get_shortened_path("mat_meth/contamination/table_res_biocides.jpg"))
```

Among herbicides, in addition to diflufenican, linuron and 3,4-dichloroaniline (3,4-DCA) were notably detected in the Loire and Gironde estuary, respectively, and are also highly prioritized for monitoring. Some other herbicides were found at elevated levels in only one estuary but remained below their PNEC values and were assigned a lower monitoring priority.

For antifouling agents, particularly marked results were observed. Dichlofluanide was found at concentrations exceeding its PNEC in the Seine estuary, while being negligible in other estuaries. Similarly, irgarol was found at elevated levels in the Gironde estuary, although it was not highly prioritized in the national monitoring framework.

Among fungicides, azoxystrobin ranked highest in monitoring prioritization and exceeded its PNEC in the Seine estuary. Tebuconazole also received a high priority ranking and was found at the highest concentrations in the Gironde and Loire estuaries, although levels remained below the PNEC. Thiram exceeded its PNEC in both the Gironde and Seine estuaries.
Overall, the Seine estuary showed the highest number of compounds exceeding PNEC values (n = 6), while the Gironde estuary exhibited the highest exceedance levels for all compounds concerned (n = 5).



# Discussion

## Surfaces de vasières

### Gironde

Les cartes des fonds sédimentaires de la Gironde de 1960 et 1998 montrent que les fonds n'ont quasiment pas varié en termes de faciès sédimentaires, hormis le chenal aval proche de l'embouchure [@baronVariabilitePhysiqueLestuaire2020]. Des travaux plus précis de @sottolichioEvaluationRecentMorphological2013 sur la distribution of lateral intertidal areas per longitudinal segment of 10 km in the Gironde estuary, derived from interpolated bathymetries of 1962, 1970, 1980 and 1994 ont permis de mesurer plus précisément l'évolution de ces surfaces en zones polyhalines et mésohalines.


##### References

::: {#refs}
:::
